utterances: But I would add that it does have tremendous potential because it does put financial and economic issues in the mix of the theological discussion.
And that's very important.
And it's very different from the civil rights movement that particularly dealt with sociopolitical issues disconnected from financial and economic matters.
So it has tremendous potential.
Now, tithes and offerings are common practices not just in churches but in synagogues, mosques, you know, any number of places of worship.
What's the difference between, you know, a tithe and what some people are looking at as potential giving that goes straight into the pockets of the minister?
Well, again, as Mr. Williams discussed, one could make a viable argument in saying that this is a matter of faith - tithing and giving above the tithe - even if it goes into the minister's pocket.
You see, what's really at stake is two questions.

To what degree - whenever you're talking about church-state relations — to what degree do I abdicate my rights as a citizen when I'm exercising my faith even as it pertains to my giving and giving for large homes, mansions, yachts?
To what degree do I abdicate my citizenship when I am a part of a parish ministry, as a parishioner?
And I think — I don't know necessarily if we can answer that question from an outside point of view.
It's really up to the individual to hold the preacher or the pastor or the parish ministry that he or she is a member accountable to where the money is going and the expenditures of that money.

Are you saying then that there should be no — because part of this is tax law, and that there should be no hand of the government in saying, well, you know, okay, you were asking people for this extraordinary amount of money, you're saying that you deserve a tax-exempt status because it's going to the, you know, the religious good, but if it's going in to your pocket.
And let me just frame this around some interviews that Creflo Dollar has given, Minister Creflo Dollar who heads the World Changes Church International in Atlanta.
The Atlanta Journal Constitution reported in 2006 his church took in $69 million.
And he says in interviews that he's got plenty of investments and book deals that get him individual money, but the church did by him a Rolls Royce that he uses for official functions.

How do you parse out the difference between that individual money and the money that is being given to the church, given that the church itself has a tax-exempt status, which relies on a certain construction of where that money is going?
Well, that's a difficult question, and I would respond by using the words of Tertullian, who says that the image of Caesar is money, the image of God is man.
Give money to Caesar, give yourself to God.

If in fact that these churches, these megachurches or the pastors owe money to the government, then the government should employ every reasonable and responsible and legal effort to retrieve the money.
But if the Senate — and I agree with Kenneth Copeland that that's an IRS issue.

But if the Senate Finance Committee is going to reconsider whether or not the churches who have amassed tremendous sources should be tax exempt, then that's going be a difficult argument for them to make particularly in America when the wealthiest of Americans and corporations are given every single opportunity to exploit tax loopholes, receive tax incentives, tax benefits, and the like.

So, I would certainly say that the government, really, in this particular matter doesn't have any moral authority or moral indignation considering that billions of dollars go unaccounted for at the federal level due to waste, pork barrel spending and bureaucratic incompetence.
So, I would use the words of Jesus here as it pertains to the government in this matter.
You know, before you try to get the toothpick out of your brother's eye, you should get the plank out of your own.
Well, Professor Epps, thank you so much.
Thank you, (unintelligible).
Professor Stephan Epps is a professor of religion and philosophy at Medgar Evers College in Brooklyn, New York.
He joined us from our New York studios.
From NPR News, this is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED.
I'm Robert Siegel in Washington.
And I'm Madeleine Brand in California.
The prospect of a pardon may have helped to ease Nixon out of the White House.
It may also, as Ford said, help to end the long nightmare.
It also undoubtedly contributed to Ford's defeat in the 1976 election.
Trista Wright spent her spring break last week as a volunteer in New Orleans gutting hurricane-damaged homes.
On Wednesday, deep in moldy, crumbling sheetrock, she stumbled upon a hidden treasure.
We spoke to Trista Wright by cell phone while she was on her way back to school in Savannah, Georgia.
So tell us what you found.

Well, we were mudding out a house, and we had just torn down the sheetrock in a bedroom, and a friend of mine, Duane, and I were raking out the sheetrock, and I started raking out a closet where an air conditioner vent had used to be, and I raked out a lot of $100 bills.
I bent down and picked it up and I thought it was Monopoly money from a board game, and we realized it was real money, so we called our pastor over, and we began raking out a whole lot more.
How much did you end up pulling out?
I think they told me it was approximately $30,000.
That's what I've been told.
So what did you do with the money?
Well, the lady who we were cleaning out the house for was out in the yard, so we notified her, and she was very excited.
Now the owner of the house has asked to remain anonymous, but...
Yes, ma'am.
...can you tell us what the story behind the money was?

Apparently, this is her parents' home, and they had passed away recently, and she pretty much inherited the house, and over the last three years, she'd been taking care of her mother in the house, and her mother had never mentioned anything to her about it.
So she passed away not too long ago and they knew that she had a lot of money, but it wasn't in her bank account, and so they didn't they never knew what happened to it till a couple of days ago.
Was there any explanation of why it would have been in the wall and not in a bank?

Well, it was old money, and she had said that her parents, after the Depression and all, they were a little, a little untrustworthy of the bank system and everything, so they would just keep their money in different places and stuff.
Problem was they forgot to tell their daughter.
Yes.
Was she excited to hear about the money?
Did she need it?
Well, the home had been severely damaged so it was a blessing for her.
She was speechless at first, but she was excited, and we were talking, and she could've hired people to come and mud out her house and bulldozer it down, and then she never would've known.
And it sounds like you guys weren't even tempted to pocket any of it, huh?
Oh, no, ma'am.

So many people have asked me about that, and they've been like, that was so nice of you not to have kept any of it, and you know, if I'd stolen the money, it would have been a temporary happiness and the (unintelligible) of my soul would have regretted it.
Trista Wright is a freshman at Armstrong Atlantic State University in Savannah, Georgia.
Thanks for talking with us, Trista.
Thank you very much, ma'am.
God bless you.
This is Talk of the Nation.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington, and here are the headlines from some stories we're following here today at NPR News.
Republican John McCain campaigned in Missouri today, where he labeled his Democratic rival Barack Obama a Liberal.
Senator Obama was in Missouri on Saturday, and plans to campaign in Florida which started early voting today.
And the defense rested today in the case against Alaska Senator, Ted Stevens, a corruption trial.
The Republican law maker is accused of lying on the Senate forms about more than $200,000 in renovations and other gifts from an oil-services contractor.
Details on those stories and of course, much more later today on All Things Considered.
Tomorrow on Talk of the Nation, the case of Troy Davis.
He's back on death row again.
Eyewitnesses identified him as a cop killer 19 years ago, but most of them later were recanted or changed their story.
The Supreme Court denied his final appeal, how is this fair?
Join us tomorrow on Talk of the Nation from NPR News.
And now the Talk of the Nation opinion page.
On ""Meet the Press"" yesterday, Colin Powell made his presidential endorsement.
The former chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff and secretary of state, said he will vote for Barack Obama.

General Powell said he admires John McCain, but he called the Republican presidential candidate unsure on the economy, and said his running mate is not ready to be president of the United States, and he had this to say about his own party.
I have some concerns about the direction that the party has taken in recent years.
It has moved more to the right than I would like to see it, but that's a choice the party makes.
I also believe that on the Republican side, over the last seven weeks, the approach of the Republican Party and Mr. McCain has become narrower and narrower.
General Colin Powell yesterday on NBC's ""Meet the Press."" We want to hear from Republicans today.
Do you agree with General Powell, and no matter who wins the election, how will the future of the Republican Party be decided?
800-989-8255, email us talk@npr.org.
And we'll start with Mickey Edwards, a former Republican congressman from Oklahoma.
Once a member of the Republican leadership, now a lecturer at Princeton University.
He's been kind enough to join us here in Studio 3A today.
Nice to have you on the program.
Thanks Neal.
Good to be here.
Hi.
Good afternoon.
